quotes.verbatim_quote: These three-note chords contain the root, 3rd, 7th (or the 6th) of each chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the foundational definition of a shell chord's intervallic content, establishing which chord tones are included and why they are harmonically sufficient.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Because of this, you hit the essential chord tones for each shape, and mostly on the
lower String sets.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This explains the practical voicing characteristic of shell chords — their placement on lower strings — which directly informs how the player positions and selects these shapes.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Since these shapes are lower on the neck than other chord shapes, you need to keep
your tone in mind so you don't clash with the bass player in your playing.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is an explicit rule about ensemble awareness: the low-register voicing requires conscious tone management to avoid muddying the bass player's frequency space.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
With a soft tone, these chords are highly effective when comping in a duo, trio, or
larger ensembles.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives the player a concrete prescription — soft tone — and the specific contexts (duo, trio, larger ensemble) where shell chords are the preferred comping tool.
